recurring_issues.pattern: Zero named citations — 'research shows' used without attribution across every article
recurring_issues.impact: QAPEDS P (Proof) Evidence Presence capped at ≤1 in all articles; AI Citability Statistical Density scores range 15–20/100 cluster-wide; no article can pass the Authority threshold for AI citation

recurring_issues.pattern: No canonical direct-answer block in the first 150 words — intros use narrative anecdote or story framing instead
recurring_issues.impact: Both QAPEDS gates (Explicit Question and Direct Answer) fail for most articles; AI systems cannot extract a citable answer; AIO readiness suppressed to 17%

recurring_issues.pattern: No first-hand practitioner experience signals — all classroom scenarios are hypothetical or illustrative fiction
recurring_issues.impact: Experience sub-component of E-E-A-T is near zero across the cluster; content reads as AI-generated filler rather than practitioner-backed guidance

recurring_issues.pattern: Zero FAQ-structured sections — no Q&A blocks exist in any article
recurring_issues.impact: Perplexity readiness is 0% across the entire cluster; FAQPage schema cannot be applied; Perplexity and Gemini citation rates are effectively zero

recurring_issues.pattern: Statistical density is critically low or zero — no percentages, effect sizes, sample sizes, or quantitative data points
recurring_issues.impact: Statistical Density sub-component scores 15–20/100 in most articles; GEO research shows named statistics increase AI citation rates by +33%

recurring_issues.pattern: Brand voice non-compliance — title case headings, em dashes, and vocabulary violations present across multiple articles
recurring_issues.impact: 7 of 12 articles have brand FAIL verdict; no article in the cluster achieves brand PASS; cluster cannot be published as-is

cluster_recommendations.issue: Universal citation and evidence failure — 'research shows' used without attribution in all 12 articles
cluster_recommendations.fix: Replace every instance of 'research shows,' 'studies suggest,' and 'research from [vague org]' with named citations in 'According to [Author, Year]' format. Minimum 3 named sources per article. Recommended sources for scaffolding: Vygotsky (1978) Zone of Proximal Development, Wood, Bruner & Ross (1976) scaffolding theory, NAEYC position statements, Sweller (1988) Cognitive Load Theory, RAND Education & Labor reports on reading/ELL instruction, What Works Clearinghouse intervention studies. Statistical targets: at least 2 quantitative data points per article (percentages, effect sizes, or sample sizes).

cluster_recommendations.issue: No canonical direct-answer blocks — AI systems cannot extract citable answers from any article intro
cluster_recommendations.fix: Rewrite every article intro to follow this structure: (1) explicit interrogative question anchoring the article within the first 120 words, (2) a 1–3 sentence canonical answer (40–60 words) that is structurally isolatable before the first H2. Example pattern: 'Scaffolding in education is [definition]. It works by [mechanism]. Teachers use it to [outcome].' This single change will unlock AIO readiness and improve QAPEDS A scores across the cluster. Target: intro ≤80 words total, direct answer block ≤60 words.

cluster_recommendations.issue: Zero FAQ sections — Perplexity readiness is 0% cluster-wide
cluster_recommendations.fix: Add a 'Frequently asked questions' section (sentence case) to every article with 4–5 Q&A pairs. Each H3 question heading must be followed by a 40–60 word self-contained answer. This enables FAQPage schema implementation across all 12 articles and is the single highest-leverage change for Perplexity and Gemini citation rates. Suggested FAQ questions should mirror actual search queries (e.g., 'What is the difference between scaffolding and differentiation?' for core articles).

cluster_recommendations.issue: No first-hand practitioner experience signals — all classroom examples are hypothetical fiction
cluster_recommendations.fix: Add at minimum one real practitioner signal per article. Options: (a) recruit 1–2 teachers from Wayground's vetting network (~30-teacher review loop) to contribute a named quote with title and school type, (b) reference Wayground's internal usage data ('Based on X teachers using scaffolding activities on Wayground...'), or (c) cite a classroom-tested outcome from a named educator in the field. Hypothetical scenarios ('Picture your Algebra class...') must be either removed or clearly labeled as illustrative, not presented as real-world evidence.

cluster_recommendations.issue: Brand compliance failures — 7 FAILs, 5 WARNs, zero PASSes across the cluster
cluster_recommendations.fix: Run a cluster-wide brand pass before any article is published: (1) convert ALL headings to sentence case — capitalize only the first word and proper nouns, (2) remove every em dash from body copy and replace with a comma, colon, or period, (3) audit vocabulary for prohibited terms ('quiz tool,' 'kids,' 'modifications,' 'SPED') and substitute per the mandatory vocabulary table, (4) check that 'Wayground' is used throughout — never 'Quizizz' except on first mention with the approved parenthetical. Item 76 requires a full content audit for keyword-topic mismatch before brand review can apply.

narrative:
# Scaffolding Cluster — GEO/SEO Analysis Report

## Cluster Overview

The Scaffolding cluster (12 articles, 4 sub-clusters) is the weakest performing group evaluated to date, with an average GEO Composite of **47.5/100** and an average QAPEDS of **4.57/10** — both well below the publishable threshold of 70+ GEO and 6.0+ QAPEDS. The cluster's defining characteristic is systemic: every article shares the same four structural deficits (no citations, no direct-answer blocks, no FAQ sections, no practitioner signals), which means this is a **template-level problem**, not an article-by-article problem. Not a single article in the cluster has achieved a brand PASS verdict.

---

## Score Summary Table

| Dimension | Cluster Avg | Target (70+) | Gap |
|---|---|---|---|
| AI Citability | 46.8 | 70 | -23.2 |
| E-E-A-T | 40.1 | 70 | -29.9 |
| Platform Optimization | 38.4 | 70 | -31.6 |
| Brand Voice | 47.1 | 70 | -22.9 |
| SEO Structure | 53.5 | 70 | -16.5 |
| Schema Recommendations | 76.5 | 70 | **+6.5** |
| **GEO Composite** | **47.5** | **70** | **-22.5** |
| **QAPEDS** | **4.57/10** | **6.0** | **-1.43** |

Schema Recommendations is the only dimension above target — articles are structurally eligible for schema markup, but the content inside them doesn't yet support it. Platform Optimization and E-E-A-T are the most critical gaps, both averaging below 41.

---

## Top 3 Recurring Issues

### 1. "Research shows" without a source — 12 of 12 articles

Every single article in this cluster uses unattributed generic research language. Phrases like "research shows," "studies suggest," and "research from cognitive scientists" appear throughout with no author, year, institution, or study name attached. Per the QAPEDS rubric, this caps the Evidence Presence sub-score at ≤1/10 — effectively nullifying the Proof dimension cluster-wide. Per GEO research, named statistics increase AI citation rates by +33%. This is the single most damaging pattern in the cluster and the highest-priority fix.

### 2. No canonical direct-answer block in the first 150 words — 11 of 12 articles

Article intros across this cluster consistently open with narrative anecdotes ("Picture your Algebra class..."), hypothetical scenarios, or general framing paragraphs. None deliver a clean, extractable 1–3 sentence definition or answer before the first H2. This causes both QAPEDS gates to fail simultaneously: the Explicit Question Gate and the Direct Answer Gate. The cascading effect: Q scores capped at ≤5, A scores capped at ≤5, and AIO readiness drops to 17% (only articles 71 and 72 pass). AI systems — Google AIO, ChatGPT, Perplexity, Gemini — require a structurally isolatable answer block to surface content as a citation. Without it, these articles are invisible to AI-generated answers.

### 3. No first-hand practitioner experience signals — 11 of 12 articles

No article in the cluster contains a named teacher quote, a named school scenario, a "we tested with X educators" signal, or first-party Wayground usage data. All classroom examples are explicitly hypothetical. This suppresses the Experience sub-component of E-E-A-T to near zero in every article and is the primary driver of the cluster's 40.1 average E-E-A-T score — the second-lowest dimension. This pattern also flags content as likely AI-generated to quality raters and AI citation systems.

---

## Platform Readiness

| Platform | Ready Count | Ready % |
|---|---|---|
| AIO Ready | 2 of 12 | 17% |
| ChatGPT Ready | 2 of 12 | 17% |
| Perplexity Ready | 0 of 12 | **0%** |

Perplexity readiness is zero across the entire cluster — no article contains FAQ-structured sections, which is the primary trigger for both FAQPage schema and Perplexity citations. The two AIO-ready articles (71 and 72) pass on structural grounds but remain limited by weak citation density. Gemini and Bing Copilot readiness is similarly low, with only articles 71 and 72 passing either. The cluster is effectively invisible to AI-answer surfaces in its current state.

---

## Brand Compliance

| Verdict | Count |
|---|---|
| PASS | 0 |
| WARN | 5 |
| FAIL | 7 |

Zero articles achieve brand PASS. This is the most alarming cluster-level brand result possible. The most common violations across FAIL and WARN articles are:

- **Title case headings** instead of required sentence case (items 80 and others)
- **Em dashes in blog copy** — a zero-tolerance violation per the brand guide
- **Vocabulary violations** — non-compliant terms present in multiple articles
- **Item 76 (scaffolding for ELLs)** has an additional critical issue: a confirmed keyword-topic mismatch (the evaluator flagged the article as targeting "scaffolding for reading" content despite the ELL title). This article requires a full content rewrite before brand or GEO review can be meaningfully applied.

No article in this cluster should be published until brand issues are resolved.

---

## Cluster-Level Recommendations

### Rec 1 — Replace all unattributed research language with named citations (All 12 articles)
This is the cluster's highest-leverage fix. Every "research shows" must be replaced with "According to [Author, Year]" or equivalent. Minimum three named sources per article. For scaffolding content, the canonical sources are available and well-documented: Vygotsky (1978) on Zone of Proximal Development, Wood, Bruner and Ross (1976) on scaffolding theory, Sweller (1988) on Cognitive Load Theory, NAEYC position statements on early childhood, and What Works Clearinghouse intervention reports for subject-specific articles. Each article also needs at least two quantitative data points — percentages, effect sizes, or sample sizes from these sources.

### Rec 2 — Rewrite every intro to include a canonical direct-answer block (11 of 12 articles)
Adopt a cluster-wide intro template: one explicit interrogative question within the first 120 words, followed immediately by a 1–3 sentence answer (40–60 words) that can stand alone without surrounding context. The entire intro must stay at or under 80 words. This single structural change will move AIO readiness from 17% toward 70%+ and will lift QAPEDS A scores across the board.

### Rec 3 — Add FAQ sections to every article to unlock Perplexity readiness (All 12 articles)
Every article needs a "Frequently asked questions" section (sentence case) with 4–5 Q&A pairs. Each question is an H3 heading; each answer is 40–60 words, self-contained, and directly responsive. This is the prerequisite for FAQPage schema and the primary driver of Perplexity citation rates. Questions should mirror actual search queries relevant to each sub-topic.

### Rec 4 — Source at least one named practitioner signal per article (11 of 12 articles)
Wayground's ~30-teacher vetting network is the most credible and accessible source for this. A single named quote per article — "[Name], [Title], [School Type]" — is sufficient to materially lift the Experience sub-component of E-E-A-T. Alternatively, Wayground's internal usage data ("Based on [X] teachers using scaffolding activities on Wayground") can serve as a first-party authority signal. Remove or clearly label all hypothetical classroom scenarios as illustrative rather than presenting them as real evidence.

### Rec 5 — Run a cluster-wide brand pass before any article is published (All 12 articles)
The brand failures are systematic and fixable with a checklist review: (1) convert all headings to sentence case, (2) remove all em dashes from body copy, (3) audit for prohibited vocabulary, (4) confirm "Wayground" is used correctly throughout. Item 76 must be triaged first — the keyword-topic mismatch means the content needs to be realigned to the ELL scaffolding topic before any other optimization applies.

---

## Standout Articles

**Strongest — Item 72: "Types of scaffolding in education" (GEO: 57.9)**
The cluster's top scorer, though still 12 points below the publishable threshold. It achieves ChatGPT and AIO readiness, and has slightly better structural organization than peers. It shares all the same core deficits but does them less severely.

**Weakest — Item 76: "Scaffolding for ELLs" (GEO: 37.3)**
The cluster's lowest scorer by a significant margin, with an additional disqualifying issue: a confirmed keyword-topic mismatch flagged by the evaluator (the article covers reading scaffolding content rather than ELL scaffolding). At 37.3 GEO and a brand WARN, this article is not a revision candidate — it requires a full rewrite aligned to the ELL scaffolding topic before any optimization work begins. Its SEO Structure score of 15/100 is the lowest individual dimension score in the entire cluster.
